summary: Gruber argues that Apple should hold two separate events rather than announcing the iPhone and iPad mini at the same September 12 event. He reasons that the iPhone is too important a product — potentially the most profitable single product in the world — to share the spotlight with any other announcement. He suggests Apple would be better served with a dedicated iPhone/iOS 6 event in September, followed by a traditional iTunes/music event in October for the iPad mini, new iPods, and possibly a rewritten iTunes app. He respectfully pushes back on Rene Ritchie's well-sourced reporting, noting that Jim Dalrymple's confirmation only covered the iPhone portion of the scoop.
key_points: The iPhone alone generates more revenue than all of Microsoft's businesses combined, making it arguably the most profitable product in the world
Apple's interest lies in keeping iPhone attention undiluted — sharing the stage with an iPad mini would split media coverage and reviewer focus
Gruber proposes two events: a September iPhone-only event followed by an October iTunes/media event for iPad mini, iPods, and possibly a rewritten iTunes app
He subtly notes that Jim Dalrymple's famous 'Yep' confirmation only quoted the iPhone portion of Ritchie's report, not the iPad mini claims
The iPad mini and new iPods make more sense grouped as an iTunes media consumption story rather than competing with the iPhone for attention
money_quotes: You'd be on solid ground arguing that the iPhone is the single most profitable product in the world today, and without question, it is the most important single product in Apple's lineup.
Apple's iPhone business alone is larger than all of Microsoft's businesses combined.
The iPhone is too big, too cool, and garners too much attention — and it's in Apple's interest to keep that attention undiluted.
An event where the iPhone shares the stage? That I'm not buying.
I don't think Apple would want reviews of both a new iPhone and new-size iPad appearing at the same time. Why share the spotlight?
And if it's an 'iTunes' event, maybe a major new version of the long-overdue-for-a-major-rewrite iTunes app? A boy can dream.
key_insight: The iPhone is too dominant and too important to Apple's business to share its announcement stage with any other product, no matter how significant.
